era_context: Classical Greece in the early decades following the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). Athens is consolidating its naval dominance and beginning its rise to hegemonic power in the Aegean. The Delian League, nominally a defensive alliance, is forming or consolidating. Sparta remains the dominant land power in Greece. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is transitioning toward the Warring States period; regional kingdoms are fragmenting into smaller, competing polities. In Persia, Xerxes I rules the Achaemenid Empire.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (no written records for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman contact
Mesoamerica (limited archaeological dating precision for this specific year)

events.title: Cimon's campaigns in the Aegean and consolidation of Athenian naval power
events.description: Cimon, son of Miltiades, leads Athenian and allied forces in campaigns against Persian-held territories and pirate strongholds in the Aegean. These campaigns, part of the broader post-Persian Wars consolidation, strengthen Athenian control over the Delian League and establish Athens as the dominant naval power. The capture of Eion and operations against Thracian territories extend Athenian influence northward. These victories cement Cimon's political dominance at Athens and fund the city's growing imperial ambitions.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.98–99)

events.certainty_note: Cimon's campaigns are well-attested in later sources, but precise dating within the 470s BCE is uncertain. Thucydides places them after the Persian Wars but does not give exact years. Plutarch's biography is written ~600 years after the events. The year 475 is conventional assignment based on historiographic consensus.
cross_references: Part of broader Delian League consolidation (-478 onwards)

events.title: Possible founding or formal reorganization of the Delian League
events.description: The Delian League, a defensive alliance of Greek city-states against Persian aggression, is either founded or formally reorganized around this time. The league's treasury is initially kept on the island of Delos, hence the name. Athens provides the military leadership and gradually transforms the league into an instrument of Athenian imperial power. The exact date of the league's founding is disputed; some sources place it in 478 BCE, others in 477 BCE. By 475 BCE, it is certainly operational and expanding.
key_figures: Aristides the Just (Athenian statesman, associated with league organization)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.96–97)

events.certainty_note: The Delian League's founding is traditionally dated to 478 or 477 BCE by ancient sources, but the exact year is uncertain. Thucydides does not provide a precise date. Modern scholarship places it within a few years of the Persian Wars' end (480 BCE). Assignment to 475 BCE is conventional and may be off by 2–3 years.

events.title: Continued fragmentation of Chinese regional states during Spring and Autumn period
events.description: In China, the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BCE) is in its final decades. The nominal Zhou dynasty has lost effective control; regional kingdoms (particularly Qi, Chu, Jin, and Qin) compete for dominance. Warfare is becoming more frequent and organized. The period is characterized by the rise of powerful feudal lords (ba, or 'hegemons') who claim to act in the Zhou king's name while pursuing their own territorial ambitions. This era produces the philosophical schools of Confucianism and Daoism in response to the chaos.
key_figures: Duke Jing of Qi (ruler of Qi state)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE
Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals), traditional dating to Confucius

events.certainty_note: The Spring and Autumn period is well-documented in Chinese chronicles, but specific events for individual years are often legendary or symbolic. The Shiji was compiled ~400 years after these events. Archaeological evidence confirms the period's general characteristics but rarely pinpoints specific years.
cross_references: -476-002 (traditional end of Spring and Autumn period)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating and significance of Cimon's early campaigns are debated. Some modern scholars argue that the major campaigns traditionally assigned to the 470s BCE may have occurred later, in the 460s BCE, based on reassessment of Thucydidean chronology. The Delian League's founding date is contested: Thucydides suggests 478 BCE, but some scholars propose 477 BCE or even later. The league's transformation from a defensive alliance to an Athenian empire was gradual, not sudden, and attributing it to a single year is anachronistic. The Spring and Autumn period's end date (traditionally 476 BCE) is itself a historiographic convention; the transition to the Warring States period was gradual, not a discrete event.
historiographic_note: For Classical Greece, sources are relatively abundant but written centuries after the events (Thucydides ~50 years later, Plutarch ~600 years later). The bias toward Athenian and elite perspectives is pronounced; we know far less about non-Athenian Greek states or the experiences of non-elites. For China, the Shiji is the primary source, compiled during the Han dynasty (~400 years after the Spring and Autumn period). It blends historical record with legend and moral instruction. Archaeological evidence from both regions is fragmentary and does not always align with textual accounts. The year 475 BCE itself is a modern convention; ancient sources rarely provide precise year-by-year dating.

graph_edges.note: Cimon's campaigns continued to expand Athenian power throughout the 470s BCE
The Delian League eventually became the Athenian Empire; treasury moved to Athens in 454 BCE
Continuation of Spring and Autumn period fragmentation
